cases.title: Choking at Dinner
cases.presentation: A 45-year-old man at a restaurant suddenly grabs his throat, cannot speak or cough, and is turning red. He is standing and responsive.
cases.question: Following the 2025 Guidelines, what should you do?
options: A: Encourage him to keep coughing.
B: Alternate 5 back blows between the shoulder blades with 5 abdominal thrusts until the object clears or he becomes unresponsive.
C: Perform a blind finger sweep.
cases.explanation: He has severe obstruction (cannot speak or cough) and is responsive. The 2025 sequence is alternating 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object is expelled or he becomes unresponsive.
cases.clinicalPearl: The 2025 update adds back blows first — 5 back blows, then 5 abdominal thrusts, repeat.

cases.title: Choking Infant
cases.presentation: A 7-month-old suddenly starts choking on a piece of food, cannot cry or breathe effectively, and is responsive with a weak, high-pitched sound.
cases.question: What is the correct technique?
options: B: Alternate 5 back blows (face-down) with 5 chest thrusts (face-up), supporting the head and neck.
cases.explanation: For a responsive choking infant, alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts, supporting the head and neck throughout. Abdominal thrusts are never used in infants due to the risk of organ injury.
cases.clinicalPearl: Babies get back blows and chest thrusts — no Heimlich for infants.

cases.title: Choking Victim Goes Limp
cases.presentation: You have been giving back blows and abdominal thrusts to a choking adult, but he suddenly becomes unresponsive and slumps.
cases.question: What is your next action?
options: B: Lower him to the ground, activate the emergency response, and begin CPR — looking in the mouth for a visible object each time you open the airway.
C: Perform repeated blind finger sweeps.
D: Wait for him to wake up.
cases.explanation: When a choking victim becomes unresponsive, lower them safely, call for help, and start CPR beginning with compressions. At each airway opening for breaths, look inside and remove a visible object only — no blind sweeps. The compressions themselves may dislodge the object.
cases.clinicalPearl: Choking + unresponsive = CPR, and the compressions double as a way to expel the object.
